articles.title: Table tennis-playing robot on track to becoming world champion
articles.summary: A robot built by Sony AI is rapidly learning how to beat the world's very best table tennis players
articles.content:
A robot built by Sony AI is rapidly learning how to beat the world's very best table tennis players

Ace in action during a match in December 2025 Sony AI

Ace, an autonomous robot powered by AI, cutting-edge sensors and an extremely dexterous arm with eight joints, has played competition-rule table tennis and beaten elite human competitors. The robot is the first machine to excel at the sport.

It was the cerebral game of chess that was first disrupted by computers, but Ace’s success suggests physical sports may be about to have their “Deep Blue” moment – the day, in 1997, that a machine of that name beat world chess champion Garry Kasparov.

“Games have long served as benchmarks for AI, including chess for Deep Blue, but also other games in more recent breakthroughs, like [the Go-playing AI] AlphaGo,” says Peter Dürr at Sony AI, Zurich Switzerland, who led the team that built Ace.

Advertisement

But he says those earlier AI milestones were played out online. Ace represents an important advance because it has taken on real-world, professional table tennis champions and held its own.

“Ace offers something that has simply never been captured before: a robot and a human in genuine athletic competition,” says Dürr.

Free newsletter Sign up to The Weekly The best of New Scientist, including long-reads, culture, podcasts and news, each week. Sign up to newsletter

Ace boasts three main advancements in autonomous robotics, he says. Firstly, it uses “event-based sensors”, which means that the robot focuses on certain regions of the images its cameras capture – those indicating changes in motion or brightness, which are critical to tracking the path of the table tennis ball.

Next, the robot’s table tennis skills are built using “model-free reinforcement learning”, which means, says Dürr, the robot “learns through experience in simulation rather than adopting a model of how table tennis should be played”. This process was similar to having the robot play a table tennis computer game, and the robot notched up several thousand hours of training during the process.

And finally, the team has deployed high-speed robot hardware that allows Ace to play with “human-like agility”, says Dürr. In some ways, it is even more agile than a human, because athletes require around 230 milliseconds to react, he says, whereas the total latency of Ace is only around 20 milliseconds.

Currently, the robot looks like a robot from a factory floor, and relies on a network of cameras and sensors surrounding the table tennis arena. But as the technology advances, the researchers expect Ace will eventually be embodied in a humanoid form.

For the matches played as part of a study published today, Japanese professional table tennis league rules applied as Ace competed against five elite but non-professional players, each of whom had competed for at least a decade and trained 20 hours per week. The robot also took on two professionals.

Ace lost only two of its five matches against elite players, but both of its matches against professional players. It did, however, achieve a win in one game within one of the professional matches.

Another advantage that Ace has over humans is that it does not give away any tells of its next move. On the other hand, it lacks the capacity to read any signs of the body language of humans.

“Some of the athletes involved in our experiments commented that they are usually watching their opponent’s face – which Ace does not have,” says Dürr.

Others were surprised by Ace’s ability to read the spin of their serves, despite their attempts to hide it with different motions. The robot also confounded its inventors – especially when it was able to hit balls that bounced off the net, which was not a skill it had trained for. This was a skill that just “emerged”, says Dürr.

Over the past year, since the study was completed, the team has continued to improve Ace’s abilities.

In December 2025, Ace beat a professional player for the first time, and in March 2026, Ace won matches against three more professional players: a female professional, Miyuu Kihara, who is ranked in the top 25 in the World Table Tennis ranking, as well as two male professionals, Tonin Ryuzaki and Fumiya Igarashi.

“With further improvements, it should be possible to outperform even the world champion,” says Dürr.

And improvements go both ways, he says.

“Former Olympian Kinjiro Nakamura noted that before watching Ace, he thought a certain shot was impossible, but having seen it, he believes human athletes could replicate this technique.”

articles.personality_title: Sony’s Ace robot is mastering table tennis against top players
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Sony AI has developed a robot named Ace that plays table tennis using artificial intelligence (AI), advanced sensors, and a flexible arm. While computers have beaten humans in games like chess and Go before, physical sports have been harder for machines to master.

**What happened** – Ace has competed in real table tennis matches following professional rules against experienced human players. It uses special sensors that focus on fast-moving parts of the scene to track the ball, learns by practicing in simulations rather than following preset rules, and moves with speed and agility close to or better than humans. In late 2025 and early 2026, Ace beat several professional players, including a top-25 ranked female player.

**Impact** – This is the first time a robot has performed so well in a fast, physical sport against skilled human athletes. Ace can react much faster than humans and does not give away hints about its next move. It even developed unexpected skills, like hitting balls that bounced off the net. These achievements show that AI and robotics are advancing from digital games to real-world physical challenges.

**What's next step** – The team plans to keep improving Ace’s skills, aiming for it to surpass the world champion one day. They also want to build a humanoid version of the robot so it can play without relying on extra cameras around the table. Meanwhile, human players are learning new techniques inspired by what Ace can do.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Sony’s Ace robot is breaking new ground by competing and winning against top human table tennis players using advanced AI and robotics technology.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le robot Ace de Sony maîtrise le tennis de table face aux meilleurs joueurs
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Sony AI a créé un robot nommé Ace qui joue au tennis de table grâce à l’intelligence artificielle (IA), des capteurs avancés et un bras très flexible. Alors que les ordinateurs ont déjà battu des humains aux échecs ou au Go, les sports physiques sont plus difficiles à maîtriser pour les machines.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Ace a participé à de vrais matchs de tennis de table selon les règles professionnelles contre des joueurs humains expérimentés. Il utilise des capteurs spéciaux qui se concentrent sur les parties rapides de la scène pour suivre la balle, apprend en s’entraînant dans des simulations sans suivre des règles fixes, et bouge avec une vitesse et une agilité proches, voire supérieures, à celles des humains. Fin 2025 et début 2026, Ace a battu plusieurs joueurs professionnels, dont une joueuse classée dans le top 25 mondial.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois qu’un robot réussit aussi bien dans un sport rapide et physique contre des athlètes humains qualifiés. Ace peut réagir bien plus vite que les humains et ne montre aucun indice sur son prochain coup. Il a même développé des compétences inattendues, comme frapper des balles qui rebondissent sur le filet. Ces succès montrent que l’IA et la robotique avancent des jeux numériques aux défis physiques réels.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe prévoit d’améliorer encore les compétences d’Ace, avec l’objectif qu’il dépasse un jour le champion du monde. Ils veulent aussi créer une version humanoïde du robot pour qu’il puisse jouer sans caméras externes autour de la table. En même temps, les joueurs humains apprennent de nouvelles techniques inspirées par ce que fait Ace.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le robot Ace de Sony ouvre une nouvelle voie en jouant et en battant des joueurs humains de tennis de table grâce à une technologie avancée d’IA et de robotique.
articles.personality_title_es: El robot Ace de Sony domina el tenis de mesa frente a los mejores jugadores
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Sony AI desarrolló un robot llamado Ace que juega tenis de mesa usando inteligencia artificial (IA), sensores avanzados y un brazo muy flexible. Aunque las computadoras han vencido a humanos en juegos como ajedrez y Go, los deportes físicos han sido más difíciles para las máquinas.

Usa sensores especiales que se enfocan en las partes rápidas de la escena para seguir la pelota, aprende practicando en simulaciones sin seguir reglas fijas, y se mueve con velocidad y agilidad similares o superiores a las humanas. A finales de 2025 y principios de 2026, Ace venció a varios jugadores profesionales, incluyendo una jugadora clasificada entre las 25 mejores del mundo.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que un robot juega tan bien en un deporte rápido y físico contra atletas humanos expertos. Ace puede reaccionar mucho más rápido que las personas y no muestra señales sobre su próximo movimiento. Incluso desarrolló habilidades inesperadas, como golpear pelotas que rebotan en la red. Estos logros muestran que la IA y la robótica están avanzando de juegos digitales a desafíos físicos reales.

**Próximos pasos** – El equipo planea seguir mejorando las habilidades de Ace, con la meta de superar al campeón mundial algún día. También quieren crear una versión humanoide para que pueda jugar sin cámaras externas alrededor de la mesa. Mientras tanto, los jugadores humanos están aprendiendo nuevas técnicas inspiradas por lo que Ace puede hacer.

**Conclusión en una frase** – El robot Ace de Sony marca un avance al competir y vencer a jugadores humanos de tenis de mesa usando tecnología avanzada de IA y robótica.

articles.title: Time to apply the brakes to runaway AI, says pioneer
articles.summary: If AI is “a very fast car with no steering wheel” then regulation must provide one, insists Nobel laureate and Artificial Intelligence pioneer Geoffrey Hinton, the visionary scientist widely known as the “godfather” of the self-learning tech.
articles.content:
Speaking at the Digital World Conference (DWC): AI for Social Development – co-organized by the UN Research Institute for Social Development (UNRISD) – Professor Hinton stressed that rapid advances in AI must be guided more carefully to serve societies – rather than undermine them.

“If you ever went out with a car that had no brake, boy, you are in trouble if you go down a hill,” he told delegates. “But you're in even more trouble if there’s no steering wheel.”

His remarks came during a busy week for AI policymaking, as governments and UN panels stepped up discussions on governance, inclusion and risk management, amid the growing integration of artificial intelligence across the global economy and society.

The haves and the have-nots

The pace of AI’s growth is staggering. According to UN Trade and Development (UNCTAD)’s Technology and Innovation Report 2025, the global AI market is projected to grow from $189 billion in 2023 to $4.8 trillion by 2033, an economy larger than Japan’s, built in a single decade.

Yet the capacity to build and shape it remains in the hands of just a few economies and firms, UNCTAD Acting Secretary‑General Pedro Manuel Moreno warned at the Commission on Science and Technology for Development (CSTD), also meeting this week.

That concentration risks deepening global inequalities. Doreen Bogdan‑Martin, Secretary‑General of the UN International Telecommunication Union (ITU), pointed out that generative AI adoption in the industrialized ‘Global North’ is growing nearly twice as fast as in the developing ‘Global South’.

“Left unaddressed, this is a second great divergence - widening the gap between countries shaping artificial intelligence and those merely consuming it” - Ms. Bogdan‑Martin said, adding that gaps in infrastructure, investment and capacity cannot be closed by any single country or organization alone.

This week’s flurry of international activity on AI and digital technology in Geneva and beyond, reflects the international push to ensure that all countries can benefit and regulate Artificial Intelligence as it increasingly shapes our economies, societies and daily lives.

Distinct areas of discussion are becoming clear.

While the focus of the Commission on Science and Technology for Development is on global‑level digital policymaking, discussions at the AI For Social Development Conference underscored the need for transparent, accountable and rights‑based AI governance to address risks such as bias, opaque algorithms and having large volumes of data concentrated in the hands of just a privileged few massive corporations.

Participants at the World Conference – convened by UNRISD and international NGO, the World Digital Techology Academy – examined AI’s growing role in social protection, labour markets, education and the green energy transition, while stressing the importance of protecting vulnerable groups and ensuring the benefits of technological change are shared more fairly.

Data-driven approach

Any proposals for AI governance must be data-driven and this is the fundamental work of the UN’s Independent International Scientific Panel on AI, which convened its first in‑person meeting in Madrid on Wednesday.

Opening the Scientific Panel’s first in‑person meeting in Madrid, co-chair Maria Ressa explained the group’s mandate to provide an independent, scientific and authoritative assessment of how AI systems are shaping societies.

Ressa, a Nobel Peace Prize laureate and campaigning Philippines journalist, warned that increasingly powerful AI tools are accelerating the undermining of democratic systems using “narrative warfare” in which falsehoods are manufactured and amplified at scale; the weakening of institutions such as the media and courts; and, ultimately, strategic corruption once accountability erodes.

Its findings will inform the discussions of another key UN AI initiative – the UN’s Global Dialogue on Artificial Intelligence Governance – which meets in July, also in Geneva.A worldwide discussion

The Scientific Panel’s findings which Ms. Ressa co-chairs with renowned Canadian computer scientist Yoshua Bengio will inform the discussions of another key UN AI initiative – the UN’s Global Dialogue on Artificial Intelligence Governance – which meets in July, also in Geneva.

The Global Dialogue brings together all 193 United Nations Member States, the private sector, civil society, academia and the tech world to share best practices and build common approaches to AI governance.

“The policy conversation will be science and evidence-based, pooled perspectives, scientific perspectives from a multidisciplinary lens from across the world,” says the UN Special Envoy for Digital and Emerging Technologies, Amandeep Gill.

“This is how policy discussions should be, and the UN is very proud to facilitate this first ever such confluence of science and policy in a fast-paced emerging technology.”
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories

This represents a meaningful, large-scale effort with broad societal impact focused on guiding AI development for the common good.
articles.personality_title: Global leaders call for careful rules to guide fast-growing AI technology
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Artificial Intelligence (AI) is developing very quickly and is becoming a big part of our world. Experts and governments are worried about how to control it so it helps everyone fairly and safely.

**What happened** – At a recent conference, Geoffrey Hinton, a top AI scientist and Nobel Prize winner, compared AI to a fast car without a steering wheel. He said we need clear rules, like brakes and steering, to keep AI from causing harm. Around the same time, UN groups and experts met to discuss how to make sure AI benefits all countries, not just the richest ones. They also talked about risks like bias and too much power in the hands of a few companies.

**Impact** – This is important because AI is growing into a huge global market, expected to be worth trillions of dollars soon. But only a few countries and companies currently control most of it, which could make the gap between rich and poor countries even bigger. The UN’s new scientific panel, led by Nobel winners, aims to study AI carefully and give advice to help guide fair and safe AI use worldwide.

**What's next step** – The UN will continue to bring together countries, companies, and experts in July to share ideas and create common rules for AI. This effort will rely on scientific research and global cooperation to make sure AI develops in ways that protect people and spread benefits fairly.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Global leaders and scientists are working together to create clear, fair rules that guide AI’s fast growth to protect societies and reduce inequality.

articles.title: Switchable 2D–3D display through a metasurface lenticular lens
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 22 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10318-9A full-colour 2D–3D switchable light-field display powered by a metasurface lenticular lens is proposed that is a promising solution for next-generation display technologies in both consumer electronics and commercial applications.
articles.content: a, Measurement setup for the efficiency of the MLL. Since the signal from the MLL diverges or converges depending on the input polarization state, direct measurement is challenging. Instead, we measure the intensity of the DC noise and acquire the signal intensity by subtracting it from the power of the incident light without the MLL. To create quasi-collimated light, only a few pixels are turned on, and the rays are collimated using a CCD lens. A LP and QWP modulate the incident light into the RCP state. Then, second QWP and LP block the signal after the light passes through the MLL. The efficiency of the MLL is measured for different incident angles. To minimize polarization aberrations, the two LPs and two QWPs are positioned perpendicular to the light’s propagation direction. The output DC component is measured using an illuminance meter (CA-210, Konica Minolta). b, Measured efficiency of the fabricated MLL. For normal incidence, the measured efficiencies are 26.6%, 46.4%, and 54.8% for 620 nm, 535 nm, and 460 nm, respectively.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant technological breakthrough in display technology with a novel switchable 2D-3D light-field display using a metasurface lenticular lens. This innovation promises to impact next-generation consumer electronics and commercial applications, offering broad and meaningful benefits. The article provides sufficient technical detail on the device's efficiency and measurement setup, indicating a substantive advancement rather than a trivial announcement.
articles.personality_title: New lens technology enables switchable 2D and 3D displays
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Display screens today usually show either flat 2D images or fixed 3D images. Switching easily between these two modes in full color has been difficult, limiting how we use screens in phones, TVs, and other devices.

**What happened** – Scientists have developed a new type of lens called a metasurface lenticular lens (MLL) that can switch a display between 2D and 3D views. This lens uses tiny structures to control light precisely. The research, published in the journal Nature on April 22, 2026, shows that the lens can efficiently handle different colors of light and change how images appear based on the light’s polarization.

**Impact** – This new lens design means screens can quickly switch between flat and 3D images without needing separate devices or glasses. The efficiency measurements show it works well for red, green, and blue light, which are the main colors used in displays. This technology could make future electronics more versatile and exciting, improving how we watch movies, play games, or use apps.

**What's next step** – Researchers will likely work on improving the lens efficiency even more and creating practical screen prototypes that companies can use. This could lead to new consumer products that let users choose between 2D and 3D viewing easily.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new metasurface lenticular lens can switch displays between 2D and 3D modes efficiently, opening the door to advanced screens in everyday devices.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle lentille permet d’alterner facilement entre affichage 2D et 3D
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les écrans actuels affichent généralement des images plates en 2D ou des images 3D fixes. Passer facilement d’un mode à l’autre en couleur complète a été difficile, limitant l’usage des écrans dans les téléphones, téléviseurs et autres appareils.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont créé un nouveau type de lentille appelée lentille lenticulaire à métasurface (MLL) qui peut basculer un écran entre vues 2D et 3D. Cette lentille utilise de toutes petites structures pour contrôler la lumière avec précision. L’étude, publiée dans la revue Nature le 22 avril 2026, montre que la lentille fonctionne bien avec différentes couleurs de lumière et change l’apparence des images selon la polarisation de la lumière.

**Impact** – Ce nouveau design de lentille signifie que les écrans peuvent rapidement passer d’images plates à des images 3D sans besoin d’appareils ou lunettes séparés. Les mesures d’efficacité montrent qu’elle fonctionne bien pour le rouge, vert et bleu, les couleurs principales des écrans. Cette technologie pourrait rendre les appareils futurs plus polyvalents et intéressants, améliorant notre façon de regarder films, jouer ou utiliser des applications.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont probablement améliorer encore l’efficacité de la lentille et créer des prototypes d’écrans pratiques pour les entreprises. Cela pourrait aboutir à de nouveaux produits permettant aux utilisateurs de choisir facilement entre 2D et 3D.

**En une phrase** – Une nouvelle lentille à métasurface peut basculer efficacement les écrans entre modes 2D et 3D, ouvrant la voie à des écrans avancés dans les appareils quotidiens.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva lente permite cambiar fácilmente entre pantallas 2D y 3D
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las pantallas actuales generalmente muestran imágenes planas en 2D o imágenes 3D fijas. Cambiar fácilmente entre estos modos en color completo ha sido difícil, limitando el uso en teléfonos, televisores y otros dispositivos.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos desarrollaron un nuevo tipo de lente llamada lente lenticular de metasuperficie (MLL) que puede cambiar una pantalla entre vistas 2D y 3D. Esta lente usa estructuras muy pequeñas para controlar la luz con precisión. La investigación, publicada en la revista Nature el 22 de abril de 2026, muestra que la lente funciona bien con diferentes colores de luz y cambia la apariencia de las imágenes según la polarización de la luz.

**Impacto** – Este nuevo diseño de lente significa que las pantallas pueden cambiar rápidamente entre imágenes planas y 3D sin necesitar dispositivos o gafas especiales. Las mediciones de eficiencia muestran que funciona bien para luz roja, verde y azul, los colores principales en las pantallas. Esta tecnología podría hacer que los dispositivos futuros sean más versátiles y atractivos, mejorando cómo vemos películas, jugamos o usamos aplicaciones.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores probablemente mejorarán aún más la eficiencia de la lente y crearán prototipos prácticos para que las empresas los usen. Esto podría llevar a nuevos productos que permitan a los usuarios elegir fácilmente entre modos 2D y 3D.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Una nueva lente de metasuperficie puede cambiar pantallas entre modos 2D y 3D de forma eficiente, abriendo camino a pantallas avanzadas en dispositivos cotidianos.
